chapterbody: lady came to see me who had a flawless face, as beautiful as that of a heavenly being. When we first met, I was a little in awe by her beauty.
chapterbody 3: Besides being a dazzling and charming beauty, she was highly educated and had led a rich, varied life. She was raised in an upper middle class family. However, she had a little regret in her life. At the age of thirty, she was still single.
chapterbody 5: In today’s world, some women are not interested in marriage, just as some men prefer to remain bachelors. But, that was not her case. She begged me to give her a mo-ding blessing, so she could find Prince Charming soon.
chapterbody 7: I gave her a mo-ding.
chapterbody 9: It did not work.
chapterbody 11: I gave her a second mo-ding.
chapterbody 13: Nothing happened.
chapterbody 15: A third mo-ding.
chapterbody 17: Still no luck.
chapterbody 19: She could not help but feel somewhat disappointed. A mo-ding from Grand Master Lu was always efficacious, but why was it unsuccessful in her case?
chapterbody 21: Was there a lack of pursuers?
chapterbody 23: No! The pursuers were as numerous as a school of silver carps. But many of them did not meet her high expectations, and she was too proud to stoop to someone she felt was lesser than her.
chapterbody 25: There were also a few well-matched to her, but every time they reached a discussion about marriage, accidents would happen.
chapterbody 27: One boyfriend was killed in a car accident.
chapterbody 29: One boyfriend suddenly broke his leg and became lame. Another boyfriend had a stroke.
chapterbody 31: And yet another became afflicted with muscular dystrophy.
chapterbody 33: All the people who had proposed to her either died or became injured. It was indeed very strange.
chapterbody 35: I also found this bizarre.
chapterbody 37: I later asked my Yidam, “Will she get married?”
chapterbody 39: The Yidam answered, “Yes!”
chapterbody 41: “Why is she still unmarried then?”
chapterbody 43: The Yidam answered, “Her guardian spirit is too powerful.”
chapterbody 45: “Who is her guardian spirit?”
chapterbody 47: The Yidam answered, “It is her first boyfriend. They were engaged and about to be married. He got into a car accident and died. After his death, he became her guardian spirit. He regards himself as her husband. Whenever any man tries to get close to her, he tries his best to break them up!”
chapterbody 49: Wow! I finally learned the reason! I asked this lady if these events were true, and she confirmed it. Her first boyfriend was killed in a car accident three days before their wedding.
chapterbody 51: I thought of a solution. After chanting a sutra to deliver her boyfriend, I turned his soul to the Wheel-Turning Sage King.
chapterbody 53: I performed a “boundary protection” around the lady. And then I gave her another mo-ding blessing.
chapterbody 54: After all these, she finally was getting married. She even sent me a wedding invitation. I did not attend the wedding but sent her a wedding gift.
chapterbody 56: Recently, I have heard she is pregnant. I am secretly happy for her. Hence, as long as one is able to find the “knot” and unfasten it, one’s problem will definitely be solved!
chapterbody 59: After a long period of time, her parents secretly took her to the temple to see me.

chapterbody: his is the account of a very pretty young college student. She had elegant brows and beautiful eyes, as well as ruby lips and pearly white teeth.
chapterbody 3: She had many pursuers and was always involved in romances. Being a generous spirit who was naturally poised, she found herself often in the limelight and many at her school considered her the most beautiful girl there.
chapterbody 5: But one day, her legs suddenly became limp and she was unable to stand up.
chapterbody 7: She was paralyzed!
chapterbody 9: She could only attend school in a wheelchair.
chapterbody 11: After the onset of paralysis, her friends, one by one, began to keep her at arm’s length. She understood that this was just the fickleness of human nature. People who knew her all felt sympathy for her, but the number of friends in her circle dwindled.
chapterbody 13: Despite all this, she managed to focus her energy studying and continued to get good grades in college.
chapterbody 15: Her parents took her to see many famous doctors, both western and Traditional Chinese medical doctors, but to no avail.
chapterbody 17: MRI scans showed nothing wrong with her brain, bones, nerves, muscles, or tendons.
chapterbody 19: They consulted jitong, the traditional mediums in temples, and after ingesting talismans, incense ashes and taking ablution with remnants of incense sticks, there was no improvement.
chapterbody 21: They then turned to alternative therapists such as massage therapists, chiropractors, masseuses, acupuncturists, diviners using the Book of Changes, and bone-setters. After exhausting all methods, her parents were still unable to help their beloved daughter stand up.
chapterbody 23: Why was this happening? No one knew.
chapterbody 25: Someone then introduced them to me.
chapterbody 27: Her parents wanted to come, but they encountered sarcastic comments from the dharma teacher they had taken refuge in:
chapterbody 29: “There is no need to see this person!”
chapterbody 31: “Everything about him is fraudulent!”
chapterbody 33: “I have so much disdain for this person that I cannot even mention his name!”
chapterbody 35: “He is a fake living buddha!”
chapterbody 37: “It is not right to talk about subjects of extraordinary things, feats of strength, disorder, and spiritual beings!”
chapterbody 39: The teacher her parents had taken refuge in was considered quite famous in the Buddhist circles. He did not like Grand Master Lu and thus tried to prevent them from coming to see me.
chapterbody 41: After a long period of time, her parents secretly took her to the temple to see me.
chapterbody 43: After I gave her a mo-ding, I looked at the light in my palm and saw an elderly woman with both legs entwined and immobilized by vines.
chapterbody 45: I asked my Yidam, “What does this mean?”
chapterbody 47: My Yidam replied, “Her grandmother is her guardian spirit. Since her guardian spirit’s legs are tied up, this young girl’s legs thus became limp.”
chapterbody 49: I suggested the family excavate her grandmother’s casket. When the casket was opened,
chapterbody 51: “Bingo!”
chapterbody 53: Indeed, vines had coiled around both the grandmother’s feet. They simply cut up the vines and removed them.
chapterbody 54: The first day after the vines were cut, the college girl felt strength slowly coming back to her legs.
chapterbody 56: Finally, she was able to stand up. Now how strange is this!
chapterbody 59: The lady in white said, “If I help heal you, you must take refuge in Grand Master Lu!”

chapterbody: woman brought three photographs to me and asked me to bless the man in the pictures.
chapterbody 3: I asked, “Who is he?”
chapterbody 5: The woman replied, “He is my elder brother.”
chapterbody 7: I asked, “What happened to your elder brother?”
chapterbody 9: She replied, “My elder brother has developed sensitive skin over his entire body. He cannot stop itching and scratching. Presently, from head to toe, he is covered with sores and inflamed and ulcerated skin.”
chapterbody 11: I asked, “Has he seen doctors?”
chapterbody 13: She said, “He has gotten injections, taken pills, and applied creams and ointments. They have tried antibiotics, steroids, everything. But nothing works.”
chapterbody 15: I carefully looked at the three pictures of the man, and I was greatly shocked. Indeed from head to toe, chest to back, arms to legs, there was not one spot of skin that was not severely affected. Those ulcerations would send a shudder through anyone looking upon them.
chapterbody 17: I said, “Ask him to chant the Earth God Mantra half a million times.”
chapterbody 19: The woman said, “He has already chanted a hundred thousand times.”
chapterbody 21: I said, “Just continue to chant it!”
chapterbody 23: I visualized myself transforming into my Yidam and used my hand to touch and stroke the man in the picture. The Cosmic Flow of Dharma flowed from my hand onto the photograph.
chapterbody 25: I said, “It will be done!”
chapterbody 27: The woman asked, “What will be done?” I replied, “A miracle will happen!”
chapterbody 29: It is said –
chapterbody 31: The woman’s elder brother had a dream.
chapterbody 33: He saw a lady wearing white with a dignified appearance who approached and asked him, “What is wrong with your body?”
chapterbody 35: He replied, “I have severe skin allergies.”
chapterbody 37: The lady in white said, “If I help heal you, you must take refuge in Grand Master Lu!”
chapterbody 39: He responded, “Of course. I have already chanted the Earth God Mantra one hundred thousand times. Grand Master Lu asked me to chant half a million. I am still doing my chanting!”
chapterbody 41: The lady in white took out a vase and poured out some water. She then used a branch dipped in the water to sprinkle water over his entire body.
chapterbody 43: In his dream, he felt a very cooling effect from the water which soothed his skin.
chapterbody 45: He also saw many tiny black bugs crawling out of his skin. They dropped to the ground and disappeared.
chapterbody 47: The lady in white said, “Your skin ailment will be healed, except for one spot on the back of your hand. When you finish chanting a half million times of Earth God Mantra, that spot will heal on its own.”
chapterbody 49: Then she took off.
chapterbody 51: He wanted to chase after her, but he woke up from his dream. Ever since that day, the ulcers started drying and stopped itching.
chapterbody 52: The markings on his skin gradually faded and about a month later, they were gone, except for one spot upon the back of one hand that still festered.
chapterbody 55: I said, “The lady in white was Guanyin Bodhisattva. Just go and thank the bodhisattva!”
chapterbody 57: He came to take refuge in me.
chapterbody 62: Later, that one spot upon the back of his hand also healed completely!
chapterbody 63: I asked, “Does the holy sage know about Madhyamaka (the Middle Way)?”

chaptername: 040 Descent of Taoist Spiritual Master Ziyang Zhenren
chapterbody: ne day, while meditating, my hands automatically formed the “Hook Invocation Mudra” and the “Holy Sage Mudra.”
chapterbody 3: A celestial being suddenly descended from the empty space and glided before me, and I asked in great joy, “Holy sage, who are you?”
chapterbody 5: He replied, “I am Ziyang Zhenren (True Man of Purple Yang).”
chapterbody 7: I asked, “Does the holy sage know of the inner fire practice?”
chapterbody 9: Ziyang Zhenren replied,
chapterbody 10: “It is the natural true fire inside one’s body. By practicing tu-na (inhalation and exhalation) diligently daily, the fire grows redder in the hearth and becomes as marvelous as the divine serpent.”
chapterbody 12: I asked, “Does the holy sage know about Madhyamaka (the Middle Way)?”
chapterbody 14: Ziyang Zhenren replied, “This school is most holy and divine. It recognizes equanimity without an iota of discrimination. Regardless of what buddha or bodhisattva one is, it just happens coincidentally after all.”
chapterbody 16: I asked, “Does the holy sage know of the Divine Water Practice?”
chapterbody 18: Ziyang Zhenren replied, “This is the ultimate treasure of humans. As the divine water sways in the splendid pool and merges with the inner fire, all magical and subtle dramas manifest.”
chapterbody 20: I asked, “Does the holy sage know about non-leakage?”
chapterbody 22: Ziyang Zhenren replied, “What is so wonderful about this method is that it redirects seed energy, but be cautious not to waste it. It is a method not to be taken lightly. Once you achieve this, everything else can be put to rest.”
chapterbody 24: I asked, “Does the holy sage know about the importance of abiding in precepts?”
chapterbody 26: Ziyang Zhenren replied, “I have practiced eight hundred virtuous deeds and accumulated three thousand meritorious deeds unbeknownst to others. I helped people resolve attachments and enmities. These were the vows I carried out to achieve accomplishment.”
chapterbody 28: I asked, “Does the holy sage understand Zen?”
chapterbody 30: Ziyang Zhenren replied, “This school fathoms truth through insight into the nature of mind. There exist infinite permutations of manifestations of the source, and a return to the source is ineffable. Great miraculous powers develop when one returns to one’s source.”
chapterbody 32: I asked, “How does one subjugate troubles?”
chapterbody 34: Ziyang Zhenren replied, “There is no need to subjugate troubles. True knowledge cannot be obtained forcefully. There is neither antecedence nor posterity, and at the instance of understanding this, one attains Buddhahood.”
chapterbody 36: I asked, “What is non-meditation?”
chapterbody 38: Ziyang Zhenren replied, “After realization, there is automatically non-meditation. Form and emptiness, light and darkness, they are the same. Does the duality of truth and falsehood truly exist? From ancient India to Caoxi (the mountain name of Huineng), the dharma propagates and flourishes.”
chapterbody 40: I asked, “How can one abide in neither good nor evil?”
chapterbody 42: Ziyang Zhenren replied, “Wear nothing on your mind! This mind is empty and nameless. Rest in the flow and let everything be spontaneously accomplished. Follow your nature, eat when hungry and drink when thirsty.”
chapterbody 44: I asked, “What is Endurance in the Realization that Dharmas have No Birth?”
chapterbody 46: Ziyang Zhenren replied, “Karmic cause and effect exists in the human and deva realms. When causes and conditions are exhausted, one returns to the source. All love and hatred are merely plays, dreams, and smoke.”
chapterbody 48: I said, “What you have said, I also know.”
chapterbody 50: Ziyang Zhenren said, “You know, I know, yet others do not know.” Ha ha ha!
chapterbody 52: Ziyang Zhenren laughed aloud as he took off.

chaptername: 041 Garab Dorje - the First Human Guru in the Dzogchen Lineage
chapterbody: s far as I know, the origin of Garab Dorje is as follows:
chapterbody 2: To pass one barrier after another towards the goal of True Mind,
chapterbody 3: A hundred years after the Buddha entered nirvana, Garab Dorje was born into the human world.
chapterbody 5: Before his incarnation, Garab Dorje was Semlhag Chen, one of the five hundred devas in the Trayastrimsa Heaven, whose father was the deva Sang Jiong. While still in the deva realm, Semlhag Chen attained accomplishment in Dzogchen practice.
chapterbody 7: Garab Dorje’s mother was a nun who was also the daughter of the King of Uddiyana.
chapterbody 9: This nun had a dream:
chapterbody 11: A man whose body was full of light entered into her womb.
chapterbody 13: The Five Dhyani Buddhas used a treasure vase to give her empowerment three times.
chapterbody 15: Through the immaculate conception, the princess nun became pregnant and later gave birth to Garab Dorje.
chapterbody 17: Ashamed, the nun threw the new born child into an ash pit. Three days later, when she went back to see the baby, he was still alive. She realized that he was an incarnation of a holy being and took him back to raise him.
chapterbody 19: At the age of seven, Garab Dorje debated with five hundred panditas (Buddhist teachers who were skilled in the five sciences) and won, bringing great surprise to these men of great knowledge.
chapterbody 21: Then Garab Dorje went to the Sun Light Mountain to meditate in retreat until thirty-two years old.
chapterbody 23: Vajrapani appeared to Garab Dorje to give him empowerment, allowing him to recover the memories of Dzogchen practices from his former life. In an instant, he achieved supreme perfect enlightenment.
chapterbody 25: Garab Dorje wrote down the Dzogchen teachings in verses.
chapterbody 27: Then he journeyed to the Sitavana cremation ground to teach the Dzogchen practices to many practitioners.
chapterbody 29: Manjushrimitra came to the cemetery and spent seventy-five years following Garab Dorje as his disciple and studying the most important Nyingthig (the Innermost Essence).
chapterbody 31: Finally, Garab Dorje, amidst the trembling of ground and shining of light, transformed his body into a rainbow and merged into the all-encompassing space.
chapterbody 33: Here are the focal points of Garab Dorje’s life:
chapterbody 35: 1. His life was linked to his former life.
chapterbody 36: 2. The first human guru of Dzogchen lineage.
chapterbody 37: 3. He was born through the immaculate conception.
chapterbody 38: 4. He was very eloquent in debates.
chapterbody 39: 5. Vajrapani opened his past life memories.
chapterbody 40: 6. He attained the fruition of Buddhahood in an instant.
chapterbody 41: 7. He taught in a cremation ground.
chapterbody 42: 8. He transmitted his teachings to Manjushrimitra.
chapterbody 46: The panditas gave Garab Dorje the name Prajnabhava after he won the debate. His original Sanskrit name was Prahevajra.
chapterbody 48: I wrote the following verses for Garab Dorje:
chapterbody 52: Descending into the human world after attaining enlightenment in heaven,

chapterbody 54: Helping others as well as oneself is divine.
chapterbody 57: But to surrender to the flow and help those who can be helped,
chapterbody 58: To end desires and attain No-birth require zeal and diligence,
chapterbody 59: This path is also formless,
chapterbody 63: A mani-pearl true sutra without words.

chapterbody: anjushrimitra was born into a wealthy Brahmin family and he loved studying the Buddhadharma from childhood.
chapterbody 2: Abiding in the mind, fire rises naturally,
chapterbody 3: He had studied under the five hundred panditas and learned the “eight stages of Buddhadharma”

chapterbody 5: One day, Manjushri Bodhisattva appeared to him and said, “You have learned the eight stages of Buddhadharma. Yet there is still one vehicle you have not learned.”
chapterbody 7: Manjushrimitra replied, “I am already a great pandita who has learned everything about Buddhadharma. How can there be something that I do not know?”
chapterbody 9: Manjushri said, “Only Garab Dorje has learned all stages, so you have to learn from him.”
chapterbody 11: Manjushrimitra said, “I refuse to believe that. I will challenge him!”
chapterbody 13: Thus--
chapterbody 15: At the Sitavana cremation ground, a debate took place. Unexpectedly Garab Dorje defeated Manjushrimitra.
chapterbody 17: Manjushrimitra was ashamed and wanted to find a knife to slice off his own tongue as an apology to Garab Dorje.
chapterbody 19: Garab Dorje said, “Cutting the tongue has no benefit but to increase your own karmic defilement. Instead of harming your wonderful buddha body, why don’t you gather all the Buddhadharma into a collection to benefit all beings.”
chapterbody 21: Manjushrimitra then became a disciple of Garab Dorje and followed him for seventy-five years.
chapterbody 23: One day, Garab Dorje transformed into the rainbow body to enter nirvana, and Manjushrimitra urgently supplicated to his guru for the Innermost Essence teaching.
chapterbody 25: Garab Dorje tossed down from the empty space “The Three Statements that Strike the Essential Points” which hit Manjushrimitra upon the head. From then on, Manjushrimitra attained perfect enlightenment.
chapterbody 27: Afterwards, Manjushrimitra sorted out Garab Dorje’s sixty-four hundred thousand Dzogchen verses into three series:
chapterbody 29: The Mind Series - the methods of recognizing the nature of one’s own mind.
chapterbody 31: The Space Series - the methods that enable a practitioner to remove doubts concerning the natural state.
chapterbody 33: The Instruction Series - the methods of pith-instructions.
chapterbody 35: Finally, Manjushrimitra went to the Sosaling cremation ground to the west of Bodhgaya. He taught there for one hundred and nine years. And he transmitted the entire teaching of Dzogchen to his disciple Shri Singha.
chapterbody 37: Here I will tell everyone:
chapterbody 39: Just like Garab Dorje, Manjushrimitra transformed into a body of rainbow lights and ascended into heaven.
chapterbody 41: That was not the end of it.
chapterbody 43: In order to help Padmasambhava, Manjushrimitra later incarnated into West India and was born inside a lotus, therefore a padmakumara. People referred to him as the “Latter Manjushrimitra” who taught dharma to Padmasambhava.
chapterbody 45: Thus:
chapterbody 47: A padmakumara transmitted the dharma teaching to another padmakumara.
chapterbody 49: Here are some verses I wrote for Manjushrimitra:
chapterbody 51: Not descending from the sky,
chapterbody 52: Nor from a heavenly abode,
chapterbody 53: The essence of humans and buddhas are the same,
chapterbody 55: Humans and buddhas are connected,
chapterbody 56: No matter how one manifests,
chapterbody 57: The sun also rises from the east.
chapterbody 59: Practicing diligently, water descends naturally.
chapterbody 62: Practices give rise to the same accomplishment.
chapterbody 63: Inside the lotus is the Innate Essence,

chapterbody: hen I returned to Seattle, as soon as I stepped into the airport and saw Lu Hong and Lu Jun there to greet me, I gave my two grandchildren a big hug.
chapterbody 3: Both grandson and granddaughter called out, “Ah Gong! Ah Gong!”
chapterbody 5: Who is to say that when one becomes a monk, one will no longer have family? One still loves and cherishes one’s own grandchildren.
chapterbody 7: This is because after all, grandparents love their grandsons and granddaughters most.
chapterbody 9: When I looked at Lu Hong who was still so small in stature, I wished him to grow bigger and taller soon.
chapterbody 11: When I looked at Lu Jun, I was shocked!
chapterbody 13: It turned out that Lu Jun had developed rashes on her entire body. She had been a lovely girl with fine, fair skin and rosy cheeks before, but now her entire body was covered with red, dry patches. Her head, face, hands, legs, back, and chest were speckled.
chapterbody 15: It is said that many Asian children born in the west develop this kind of skin allergy. Some outgrow it when they are older and some will not.
chapterbody 16: I had not expected Lu Jun’s skin allergies to be this severe. A once lovely face was now covered with pockmarks.
chapterbody 18: Had she seen any doctors? Yes!
chapterbody 20: Doctors prescribed her antibiotics and steroids which had side effects with prolonged use.
chapterbody 22: Doctors instructed her to avoid hot water when bathing.
chapterbody 24: Doctors instructed her to take fewer baths to avoid drying out her skin.
chapterbody 26: Doctors instructed her to wear special clothing at night to prevent scratching her itchy skin during sleep which would lead to broken skin and infections, further aggravating the condition.
chapterbody 28: (Regarding fewer baths, this is because baths cause the skin to become dry. People with skin allergies living in the continental climate zones need to apply moisturizing lotions.)
chapterbody 30: Lu Jun has seen many doctors but her skin has not healed. It was indeed terrible.
chapterbody 32: Also the doctors dictated what she could and could not eat. With a restricted diet, Lu Jun’s face became slim and long. When I saw her, I was distressed and felt badly for her!
chapterbody 34: Lu Hong and Lu Jun stayed overnight at our house one evening. Taking advantage of this opportunity, I asked both of them to come and kneel before the shrine. I placed my hand on Lu Hong and Lu Jun to give them a mo-ding.
chapterbody 36: I prayed:
chapterbody 38: Lu Hong - quickly grow taller and bigger! Lu Jun’s skin - quickly get better!
chapterbody 40: I prayed to Golden Mother of Jade Pond to bless my grandson and granddaughter.
chapterbody 42: The two of them obediently joined their palms in a presentable manner.
chapterbody 44: I also used the Four Vertical-Five Horizontal Seal to empower a cup of Golden Mother of Jade Pond holy water to give to Lu Jun to drink.
chapterbody 46: A week later, a miracle happened!
chapterbody 48: The red patches on her face disappeared, and the red patches on her neck disappeared. The red patches on her entire body also faded. What remained were only traces of the red swellings.
chapterbody 50: It will take some time before the traces of erythema become completely invisible.
chapterbody 52: This was already a very good result!
chapterbody 55: I prayed to Golden Mother of Jade Pond:
chapterbody 57: On Sunday, June 16th 2013, which was Father’s Day, Lu Jun wrote on the card for her grandfather, “Thank you Ah Gong for blessing me! Thank you Golden Mother!”
chapterbody 62: May the dharma power flow through my hands and instill itself into the body-mind of all my disciples, so they will have good health, joy, and success in cultivation!
chapterbody 63: Pain that had been present for fifteen years disappeared and he was cured after the acupuncture treatment.

chapterbody: harma brother Hong, a student from Edmonton, Canada, came with his wife to the Ling Shen Ching Tze Temple in Redmond to see me.
chapterbody 3: He had had back pain for fifteen years and seen numerous Chinese and Western medical doctors who provided temporary relief but were unable to cure him.
chapterbody 5: He read in books that years ago Grand Master Lu had suffered a brain splitting syndrome which resulted in the four great elements in his body scattering. To cure this illness, Grand Master Lu flew from Taiwan to Korea to visit the many large and small temples and Buddhist pagodas there to make prostrations and circumambulations.
chapterbody 7: Grand Master Lu circumambulated the Many Jewels Buddha Pagoda.
chapterbody 9: He made prostrations to the Vaidurya Light Medicine Buddha, a large Buddha statue.
chapterbody 11: He paid homage to the Sunlight Bodhisattva, Moonlight Bodhisattva, Medicine King Bodhisattva, Medicine Supreme Bodhisattva, and the twelve Yaksha Generals….
chapterbody 13: On his return trip to Seoul, Grand Master saw the Medicine Buddha and his retinue appearing and he was greatly moved, knowing that his brain splitting syndrome and the scattering of his four great elements would definitely be cured.
chapterbody 15: Later, Grand Master indeed recovered from his illness!
chapterbody 17: Hence, dharma brother Hong felt that there were great merits in paying homage to buddhas in temples and circumambulations around stupas and pagodas.
chapterbody 19: Dharma brother Hong flew from Canada to Japan. He also wanted to emulate Grand Master Lu by visiting the many temples and pagodas in Japan, making prostrations and circumambulations.
chapterbody 21: Similarly he made prostrations at a Medicine Buddha Hall in a temple.
chapterbody 23: That night, he dreamt of Grand Master Lu descending slowly from the sky and inserting needles in his back.
chapterbody 25: His entire back was covered with acupuncture needles. Then Grand Master Lu pulled out the needles one by one.
chapterbody 27: The next day when he woke up, his back hurt just the same. He did not get well right away.
chapterbody 29: But, the pain diminished day by day.
chapterbody 31: Several days later, the back pain was completely gone!
chapterbody 33: Pain that had been present for fifteen years disappeared and he was cured after the acupuncture treatment.
chapterbody 35: This was a miracle!
chapterbody 37: Dharma brother Hong believed that Grand Master Lu had used the method of acupuncture to heal him!
chapterbody 39: I would like to explain here:
chapterbody 41: I am neither a traditional Chinese doctor nor an acupuncturist, and I do not know anything about acupuncture.
chapterbody 43: Licensure to practice traditional Chinese medicine requires passing an examination.
chapterbody 45: Practicing acupuncture also requires one to pass an examination.
chapterbody 47: Both require licensure and are not to be attempted recklessly. But, in the dream state, where bizarre things happen, one can practice acupuncture without a license.
chapterbody 49: Also, why was Grand Master Lu, who does not know acupuncture, able to insert needles to treat someone in a dream?
chapterbody 51: The answer is very simple. I say:
chapterbody 53: As long as I am in the heavenly realm, when the three heavenly beings - Sun Virtue Exalted One, Moon Virtue Exalted One, and the Heavenly Medicine Exalted One - merge with Grand Master Lu, I become a holy heavenly healer and can naturally apply acupuncture treatment to dharma brother Hong’s back.
chapterbody 54: But, I could only insert thousands of acupuncture needles in dreams. It is not illegal to do that in dreams.
chapterbody 56: (Hahaha!)
chapterbody 58: People generally find these events or miracles very mysterious and inconceivable. In fact, these events are not dreams but reality.
chapterbody 60: He happened to run into me, and he asked me to give him a mo-ding blessing.

chapterbody: n elderly student was getting out of bed abruptly one morning, and his legs suddenly gave way and he fell flat on the floor.
chapterbody 2: The doctor asked, “Who set this for you?”
chapterbody 3: As he fell, he reflexively stretched out his arms to protect himself, and “ka-cha” a bone cracked. He cried out loudly in pain.
chapterbody 5: His family came quickly and called an ambulance to take him to the hospital.
chapterbody 7: The doctor said, “You have sustained a fracture. Other than that, everything else is fine.”
chapterbody 9: After moving pieces of the bone back into their correct position, the doctor then put a cast around his arm which was stretched upward, as if he were greeting someone.
chapterbody 11: The doctor said, “When the cast is removed, you will be healed!” The doctor said, “Elderly people should stay in bed for several minutes after waking up, waiting until they are fully awake before getting out of bed.”
chapterbody 13: The elderly may not be completely alert when they first wake up in the morning. If they get out of bed too abruptly, they may lose control of their legs and easily fall.
chapterbody 15: Situations where falls can happen include:
chapterbody 17: Getting out of bed,
chapterbody 18: Missing a step,
chapterbody 19: Slipping in the bathroom.
chapterbody 22: Get out of bed slowly. It is fine to stay in bed if you do not feel like getting out of bed right away.
chapterbody 24: When walking, pay attention to where there are steps.
chapterbody 26: When climbing stairs, grasp the handrail and take note of the steps and be sure to look where your foot will land especially the last step. Do not engage in conversations or greetings when climbing up or down stairs. It is easy to fall when distracted.
chapterbody 28: Bathrooms can be hazardous locations for injuries because floors may be wet.
chapterbody 30: One is prone to falling when climbing in and out of bathtubs. Soapsuds are slippery.
chapterbody 32: Also, avoid walking on mossy ground or rocks covered in moss or stepping across ditches etc….
chapterbody 34: After placement of the cast, the elderly student’s arm continued to hurt badly. In fact, the pain got worse day by day.
chapterbody 36: He went back to the hospital to get an X-ray and it was discovered that his bones had not been set correctly. There was a slight misalignment. Also, there was a small piece of broken bone that had not set during the first reduction.
chapterbody 38: The doctor said, “We must remove the cast and reset the bones.”
chapterbody 40: Before going to the hospital again, the elderly student came to the Lei Tsang Temple to pray that the procedure would be smooth and successful.
chapterbody 42: He happened to run into me, and he asked me to give him a mo-ding blessing.
chapterbody 44: I laid my right hand on his right hand, and I also touched his cast while chanting a mantra.
chapterbody 46: That night, Grand Master Lu appeared to the elderly student in a dream.
chapterbody 48: Grand Master Lu said, “Let me set your bones for you.”
chapterbody 50: Grand Master Lu grabbed the arm of the elderly student and, as if he were playing with it, he rotated it like a gyroscope. Then there was a sound of “ka-cha,” and the bone seemed to have been reset.
chapterbody 52: During the dream, the arm did not hurt. It was very strange.
chapterbody 55: This time the orthopedic doctor opened his eyes widely in great surprise.
chapterbody 57: The elderly student went at the appointed time to the hospital to have the cast removed. Before the adjustment, another X-ray was taken.
chapterbody 62: “The bone is set properly, and the small piece of fractured bone has also gone!”
chapterbody 63: The elderly student said, “Grand Master Lu.”

chapterbody: ere is a joke:
chapterbody 2: This time it was different. She dreamt of the “eight waters of merit” cleansing her brain, and also many copies of sutras, tantras, and treatises being installed into her brain, as well as me placing all my written books into her mind.
chapterbody 3: A man suffering from forgetfulness went to see a specialist.
chapterbody 4: She has supreme memory and can recall everything she reads now!
chapterbody 5: The doctor asked, “What kind of things do you forget?”
chapterbody 7: The patient replied, “Every time I buy something I forget to pay.”
chapterbody 9: The doctor immediately called out to the nurse, “Come quickly. Ask him to pay his medical fees first!”
chapterbody 11: The doctor then asked, “What else do you forget?”
chapterbody 13: The patient replied, “Here and there, I forget everything. Doctor, how do you treat this?”
chapterbody 15: The doctor replied, “The best method to treat forgetfulness is to forget that you have a problem.”
chapterbody 17: I have here one example:
chapterbody 19: A middle aged female student suffered from forgetfulness.
chapterbody 21: She forgot to take her medicine.
chapterbody 22: She forgot to close the door.
chapterbody 23: She forgot her glasses.
chapterbody 24: She forgot to turn off the stove. She forgot her purse.
chapterbody 25: She forgot her umbrella.
chapterbody 26: She forgot the numbers to the safe.
chapterbody 27: Etc….
chapterbody 29: She studied Buddhism and had read many scriptures. But, it was as if she had never studied or read at all. As soon as she finished reading, she forgot everything and could not recall even one sentence. This brought her great pain.
chapterbody 31: She forgot how to chant mantras.
chapterbody 32: She forgot how to form mudras.
chapterbody 33: She forgot how to perform visualization.
chapterbody 34: She forgot almost all steps in the liturgy.
chapterbody 36: When attending dharma teachings, she was present and heard the teaching at the time but soon after, it was all forgotten.
chapterbody 38: If you asked her, “What did Grand Master talk about yesterday?”
chapterbody 40: She would reply, “I have completely forgotten. My mind is blank.”
chapterbody 42: Later, she asked me to give her a mo-ding. I complied and gave her mo-ding many times, but she was still forgetful.
chapterbody 44: I drew a talisman for her, but she continued to be forgetful.
chapterbody 46: I energized water for her to drink, but it was futile.
chapterbody 48: She chanted the Akasagarbha Memory Improvement Mantra syllable by syllable and day after day, to no avail.
chapterbody 50: I was also at my wit’s end, becoming almost neurotic and blank in my mind.
chapterbody 52: One day the Venerable Ananda descended without any invocation.
chapterbody 55: The Venerable Ananda said to me, “Visualize me residing above your crown when you bless her.”
chapterbody 57: I suddenly had an inspiration. The Venerable Ananda is known for his extraordinary memory. Since Ananda does not forget anything he has heard, he must have a solution.
chapterbody 62: I gave this student another mo-ding.
chapterbody 63: The past, present, and future all emerged one by one.

chapterbody: ne night, in a dream, I saw three students kowtowing to me. Immediately afterwards, each offered me a soft boiled egg.
chapterbody 3: I asked, “Why are these eggs so strange, without any egg shells?” The three students said, “These eggs are without egg shells.”
chapterbody 5: I asked, “How did you get them?”
chapterbody 7: The three replied, “We have no idea, but they have grown inside us.”
chapterbody 9: I swallowed the three eggs.
chapterbody 11: Then I woke up from this strange dream.
chapterbody 13: The next day, three students indeed traveled from a country far away seeking mo-ding.
chapterbody 15: I asked, “What is the matter?”
chapterbody 17: The three students said, “We each have a tumor in our intestines. The doctors wanted to perform surgeries on us. We have come especially to request a mo-ding, and we will then travel back home to have the surgery and have the three tumors resected.”
chapterbody 19: I said, “You are all fine now!”
chapterbody 21: The three asked, “Why are we all fine now?”
chapterbody 23: I replied, “Last night I dreamt I’d swallowed three eggs. I believe the eggs I swallowed were your tumors. If you do not believe this, go back to the hospital for an examination. If your tumors are indeed gone, they have been eaten by me!”
chapterbody 25: “Is this true?”
chapterbody 27: “It is true.”
chapterbody 29: Later those three students went for a check-up and discovered that the cancerous intestinal tumors had indeed disappeared!
chapterbody 31: Someone asked, “Three tumors have suddenly vanished. By swallowing the three eggs, won’t Grand Master Lu be taking over their karma? Will three tumors grow in Grand Master Lu’s intestines now?”
chapterbody 33: I replied, “It won’t harm me.”
chapterbody 35: “Why?”
chapterbody 37: I replied, “I chewed those three eggs before swallowing them. They have been digested and eliminated from my body.”
chapterbody 39: It was a very strange thing. Those three eggs turned out to be three tumors.
chapterbody 43: In another incident, I dreamt I was gnawing on some bark.
chapterbody 45: It was very rough bark. But, I managed to bite and chew off the bark of an entire tree.
chapterbody 47: When I woke up, I realized it was another strange dream.
chapterbody 49: I saw a student who sought me out for mo-ding. He lifted up his shirt and trousers to show me his skin.
chapterbody 51: Oh heavens!
chapterbody 53: His skin was mottled with speckles, even the area covered by his trousers. There was not a spot of normal skin.
chapterbody 54: I said to him, “You will be healed!”
chapterbody 56: He replied, “I have had this for thirty years, and I have tried all kinds of medicines. Is it possible for me to get well?”
chapterbody 58: I said, “You will heal in three days.”
chapterbody 59: Indeed, three days later, without taking any medicine, he became well. This was because Grand Master Lu had eaten his skin affliction.
chapterbody 66: “If you are sick or have pain, you have to see the doctor first. You should do that before coming to ask me for a blessing!”

chapterbody: ase No.1
chapterbody 3: Chen Chuanfang came to the Rainbow Temple to attend a fire offering ceremony. Her knees hurt so badly that it was hard for her to walk.
chapterbody 5: She sought Grand Master Lu for a blessing. I tapped my hand upon her two kneecaps. Her knees stopped hurting.
chapterbody 7: A month later, she came again to Seattle for a dharma ceremony. She said, “My knees had hurt for many years. But after Grand Master tapped them, the pain disappeared.”
chapterbody 9: She said, “Grand Master Lu’s hands are pain killers!”
chapterbody 11: Case No. 2
chapterbody 13: The elder sister of Reverend Lily came to attend the group practice of Amitayus Sadhana on the day of my birthday.
chapterbody 15: Suddenly her back and two knees began to spasm. It was so painful that she had to squat and could not get up.
chapterbody 17: She was going to call a taxi to take her home to rest. Reverend Lily saw this and asked me to give her a blessing.
chapterbody 19: I tapped my hand once on her back and once on each kneecap. Before the Amitayus Sadhana group practice started, her back pain was gone, and the pain in her knees was also gone. She suddenly was able to stay behind to participate in the group practice.
chapterbody 21: She said, “Grand Master Lu’s hands are pain killers!”
chapterbody 23: Case No. 3
chapterbody 25: Mr. Yan Cheng woke up one day with a stiff neck. His neck hurt badly and he could not even turn it.
chapterbody 27: He tried the Salonpas pain relieving patch to no avail. He tried the Tibetan pain relieving patch to no avail.
chapterbody 29: He took analgesics and anti-inflammatory pills to no avail.
chapterbody 31: A month later, the neck was still hurting and he had also developed a rash due to sensitivity to the medicine patches.
chapterbody 33: Mr. Yan Cheng came to the Lei Tsang Temple to ask me for a blessing. I used my hand to tap his neck.
chapterbody 35: Two days later, the pain was completely gone. Even the red rash was gone.
chapterbody 37: Mr. Yan Cheng said, “Grand Master Lu’s hands are pain killers!”
chapterbody 39: Case No. 4
chapterbody 41: A Mr. Jing Xiong traveled from afar to Seattle. He developed a tooth ache while on the airplane.
chapterbody 43: As soon as he got off the plane he went to the hospital for an examination. It turned out that he had gum inflammation.
chapterbody 45: Half of his face was swollen, and he cried out in pain. After taking pain killers and anti-inflammatory pills given by the doctor, he did not get well right away. He came to ask me to give him a blessing.
chapterbody 47: I tapped once on each cheek. A miracle happened.
chapterbody 49: The inflammation stopped immediately, and the pain was gone! Mr. Jing Xiong said, “Grand Master Lu’s hands are pain killers!”
chapterbody 51: Case No. 5
chapterbody 53: A woman by the name of Mei Qi developed a sharp pain in her chest before a dharma ceremony at the Rainbow Temple. Her face turned ashen and she broke out in a cold sweat. She sought me to bless her.
chapterbody 54: I formed the Sword Finger Mudra and pointed it at her heart area from a distance. Energy was discharged into her heart for just a moment.
chapterbody 56: Her face turned rosy and she stopped perspiring. The sharp pain was also gone.
chapterbody 58: She said, “Grand Master Lu’s hands are pain killers!”
chapterbody 59: I said, “If you are sick or have pain, you have to see the doctor first. You should do that before coming to ask me for a blessing!”
chapterbody 66: I do not worry about how much more I can write, because to live is to continue writing.

chapterbody: n 2000, during the Kalachakra Dharma Ceremony in Hong Kong, Master Lianhan gave a discourse on the Kalachakra precepts.
chapterbody 2: Making comments that cause someone with faith to lose faith.
chapterbody 3: At the Kalachakra Dharma Ceremony in London (June 23, 2013), I re-stated the Kalachakra precepts.
chapterbody 5: 1. Refrain from fourteen actions which constitute a root downfall:
chapterbody 7: Having contempt for the precepts.
chapterbody 8: Scorning or deriding the Root Guru.
chapterbody 9: Faulting our vajra brothers and sisters.
chapterbody 10: Giving up love for sentient beings.
chapterbody 11: Giving up bodhicitta, both bodhicitta in aspiration and bodhicitta in action.
chapterbody 13: Slandering mantras.
chapterbody 14: Revealing confidential teachings to those who are unripe.
chapterbody 15: Abusing our five aggregates.
chapterbody 17: Being friends with malevolent people.
chapterbody 18: Deterring the faith of practitioners.
chapterbody 19: Not keeping our own vows.
chapterbody 22: 2. Observe the eighteen root bodhisattva vows and avoid doing the following:
chapterbody 24: Praising oneself or belittling others.
chapterbody 25: Not teaching the Dharma or giving material aid.
chapterbody 26: Not forgiving those who repent.
chapterbody 27: Abandoning the Mahayana.
chapterbody 28: Stealing things belonging to the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha. Abandoning the Buddhadharma.
chapterbody 29: Stealing the robes of ordained ones.
chapterbody 30: Committing any of the five extremely unpardonable sins.
chapterbody 31: Holding distorted views.
chapterbody 32: Destroying instead of repairing.
chapterbody 33: Explaining emptiness to those who are not prepared for this understanding.
chapterbody 34: Turning people away from the path towards enlightenment
chapterbody 35: Causing others to abandon the precepts.
chapterbody 36: Deriding the Hinayana.
chapterbody 37: Falsely claiming that one has realized profound emptiness.
chapterbody 38: Accepting ill-gotten money or gifts.
chapterbody 39: Deriding disciplinary rules.
chapterbody 40: Abandoning the vows to benefit others.
chapterbody 42: 3. Observe the twenty-five codes of conduct:
chapterbody 44: Avoid the five destructive actions of: killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, false speech, and taking intoxicants.
chapterbody 46: Avoid the five auxiliary destructive actions of: gambling, earning profits by misconduct, reading books and graphics that spread heresy, worshipping ancestors or ghosts and spirits using the method of sacrificing a life, and consumption of impure meat.
chapterbody 48: Avoid the five types of murder: killing men, killing women, killing children, killing cattle, and destroying representations of Buddha’s body, speech, or mind (such as temples, stupas, and statues).
chapterbody 50: Avoid the five types of negative actions: harming a buddha or teacher, harming members of the sangha, harming family and friends, harming a leader or host, and harming those who trust us.
chapterbody 52: Avoid the five longings of: sights, sounds, fragrances, tastes, and tactile sensations (eyes craving beauty, ears craving melodious sounds, nose craving nice fragrances, tongue craving good taste, and body craving sensual pleasures).
chapterbody 55: Employing unqualified mudras.
chapterbody 56: Not seeking the means to develop “concentration.”
chapterbody 57: 4. Guard oneself from the following coarse sins:
chapterbody 58: Creating disputes during dharma ceremonies.
chapterbody 59: Staying seven days at the house of a Hinayana practitioner.
chapterbody 60: Explaining Tantrayana to people who do not believe in the Buddhadharma.
chapterbody 61: During practice, one fails to attain or approach attaining “merging with deity.”
chapterbody 62: Teaching Highest Yoga Tantra to those who are not the right vessels.
chapterbody 63: Posing oneself as a yogin who practices qi.

chapterbody: A-
chapterbody 2: Buddha doctrine or the teachings of the Buddha.
chapterbody 3: Acharya (Sanskrit, literally “Teacher”)
chapterbody 4: Male counterpart of the dakini (see Dakini).
chapterbody 5: A supreme teacher within Vajrayana Buddhism. The acharya may conduct ceremonies or bestow teachings and empowerments according to the acharya’s level. Synonymous with “vajra master.”
chapterbody 7: Akasagarbha Bodhisattva (Sanskrit, literally “Repository of the Void”) One of the eight mahasattvas (great beings), the bodhisattva of wisdom, virtue, and good fortune, whose activity is to fulfill all wishes. The name of Akasagarbha Bodhisattva literally means “repository of the void,” void here indicating not merely nothingness, but the mysterious potentiality that gives rise to all phenomena.
chapterbody 9: Amitabha Buddha (Sanskrit, literally “Boundless light”)
chapterbody 10: A type of wrathful deity, whose function is to protect Buddhist practitioners.
chapterbody 11: The Buddha of Boundless Light and Longevity, he is one of the Five Wisdom Buddhas and the Lord of the Lotus Family. He embodies the Wisdom of Discerning Awareness which is the antidote to desire and lust. His color is red, element is fire, and direction is west. He is depicted with his hands forming the meditation mudra. Amitabha Buddha’s pure land (paradise) is called Sukhavati and is located in the western direction. He is the Primary Buddha of the Pure Land Sect and often depicted to be accompanied by his two attendants, Avalokitesvara Bodhisattva and Mahasthamaprapta Bodhisattva.
chapterbody 13: Ananda
chapterbody 14: Gods or heavenly beings. Due to their good merit, they are able to enjoy the pleasures of heaven but are still in the samsaric cycle and will eventually be reborn in another realm.
chapterbody 15: A devout attendant and a cousin of Shakyamuni Buddha. During the First Buddhist Council convened to preserve the Buddha’s teachings, it was Ananda who recalled and recited the majority of Shakyamuni Buddha’s discourses because of his exceptional memory. These discourses became the sutras.
chapterbody 17: Arhat (Sanskrit, literally “Worthy One, Vanquisher of Enemies”) One who has exhausted all defilements and mental afflictions that cause one to take rebirth in the six realms of samsara (the cycle of karma and reincarnation), hence is free from suffering and entered into nirvana (the state of liberation from samsara).
chapterbody 18: The most supreme teaching of the Nyingmapa sect originally taught by the Primordial Buddha Samantabhadra. Through this accomplishment, one recognizes the purity of the mind that has always been present and realizes the union of emptiness and wisdom. It is not a practice or technique – it is the mind’s original and fundamental state.
chapterbody 19: Atisa (982 AD - 1054 AD)
chapterbody 21: A great Indian Buddhist master who was responsible for reintroducing pure Buddhism into Tibet. He is the author of The Seven Points of Mind Training.
chapterbody 22: Empowerment
chapterbody 23: Avalokitesvara (Sanskrit, literally “Lord Who Observes Sounds of the World”)
chapterbody 24: A ritual wherein the guru transmits to a student the energy of a particular deity or practice so that the student's practice may quickly bear fruit.
chapterbody 25: The embodiment of compassion, Avalokitesvara Bodhisattva compassionately observes the sounds of the world and renders assistance to any devotee who calls out his name. The stories of prayers answered and the myriad miracles performed by Avalokitesvara made him the most widely worshipped bodhisattva. Known as Chenrezig in Tibet, the Tibetan people claim to be his descendents and consider him as their patron bodhisattva. They believe that Chenrezig has appeared many times in Tibet to protect the Buddhist faith. King Songtsan Gampo (the Tibetan king who introduced Buddhism into Tibet) and Dalai Lama are believed by Tibetans to be incarnations of Chenrezig. As result of this special relationship, Avalokitesvara’s mantra, “Om Mani Padme Hum,” is the most widely chanted mantra by the Tibetan people.
chapterbody 27: Avalokitesvara Bodhisattva is worshipped in China as the female bodhisattva, Guanyin. This change of Avalokitesvara from a male bodhisattva to being depicted as a female one apparently occurred gradually during the Song Dynasty. Some have postulated that Chinese worship Avalokitesvara as a female deity because Chinese culture views compassion as a feminine quality. Guanyin’s popularity in China is summed up by the Chinese saying that “The Amitabha Buddha’s name is chanted in every house. Guanyin Bodhisattva is worshipped in every home.” As Buddhism spread from China into the neighboring Asian countries such as Korea and Japan, Avalokitesvara was introduced to them as Guanyin, a female bodhisattva.
chapterbody 28: Feng Shui (Geomancy; Chinese, literally “Wind Water”)
chapterbody 29: The Buddhist scriptures speak of Avalokitesvara appearing in many forms including the two armed, four armed, or the thousand armed and thousand eyed Avalokitesvara. The scriptures also state that Avalokitesvara will appear in the most suitable form for the circum stances when rendering assistance.
chapterbody 30: Use of astronomy and geography in placement of buildings, gardens, water features, furniture, objects, etc. to help one improve life by receiving positive energy. It is an art and science which originated in China approximately three thousand years ago.
chapterbody 32: Five Buddhas
chapterbody 33: Bardo (Tibetan, literally “Intermediate State”)
chapterbody 34: Also known as Five Dhyani Buddhas, the Five Buddhas are celestial buddhas visualized in Vajrayana meditations. The five Buddhas are Aksobhya, Amitabha, Amoghasiddhi, Ratnasambhava and Vairocana. Each embodies a different aspect of enlightened consciousness to aid in spiritual transformation: (1) Vairocana Buddha – Wisdom of Ultimate Reality; (2) Akshobhya Buddha - Wisdom of Great Mirrorlike; (3) Ratnasambhava Buddha - Wisdom of Equality in Nature; (4) Amitabha Buddha - Wisdom of Discerning Awareness;
chapterbody 35: This term refers to a phase between two states. There are six instances of this, as taught by Padmasambhava: (1) bardo between birth and death - our ordinary consciousness during this present lifetime; (2) bardo of the dream state - the state we experience in sleep; (3) bardo of meditative concentration - the state of meditative stability generally experienced by accomplished meditators; (4) bardo of the moment of death - the state which begins when one begins to die and lasts until the moment when the separation of the mind and body takes place;
chapterbody 36: (5) Amoghasiddhi Buddha - Wisdom of All-accomplishing.
chapterbody 37: (5) bardo of reality (Dharmadhatu) - the first phase of the after-death experience; (6) bardo of becoming - the state where the mind moves towards rebirth.
chapterbody 39: Black and White Judges of City God Temples
chapterbody 40: Gelug/Gelugpa/Yellow Sect
chapterbody 41: Also known as Black and White Wuchang (Impermanence), two spirit guards who serve under Yanluowang or King Yama and are in charge of escorting the spirits of the dead to the Underworld.
chapterbody 42: Known as the Yellow Sect because of the large yellow hats worn by the reverends during ceremonies, this is the most popular school currently in Tibet and surrounding regions. The founder of Gelug, Tsongkhapa (1367-1419 AD) received teachings from Nyingma, Kaygu, Sakya and other schools. Tsongkhapa was well known for his strict observance of the monastic disciplines as set forth in the Vinaya. In the mid sixteenth century, Sonam Gyatso, the second reincarnation of Tsongkhapa’s main disciple was later bestowed the title “Dalai” (Mongolian for “ocean”) and the institution of Dalai Lama was created.
chapterbody 43: Bodhicitta (Sanskrit, literally “Awakened Mind”)
chapterbody 44: Golden Mother of the Jade Pond
chapterbody 45: The key to Mahayana Buddhism, it refers both to an enlightened mind and to the resolution arising for the profound compassion to attain an enlightened mind for the purpose of assisting all beings.
chapterbody 46: Ruler of all female immortals, she is the most important female deity of the Taoist Pantheon. Known by many names such as Queen Mother of the West, she came into being from the gathering of primordial yin (feminine) energy. Her palace is located on top a peak in the Kunlun Mountain Range. She represents the metal element in the Taoists Five Elements (metal, wood, water, fire, and earth) and there is a Jade Pond near her palace, hence she is also known as the Golden Mother of Jade Pond.
chapterbody 47: Bodhisattva (Sanskrit, literally “Enlightenment-being with Compassion”)
chapterbody 48: Great Compassion Dharani
chapterbody 49: One who has developed the altruistic motive of dedicating his existence throughout all rebirths to the attainment of enlightenment in order to liberate other beings who are suffering in samsara (the cycle of karma and reincarnation). In Mahayana Buddhism, there are fifty-two grounds (stages) schemata of enlightenment. It depends on one’s merits and virtues, the development of bodhisattva path stands from the forty-first to the fiftieth grounds. Sometimes, it is referring to the “Bodhisattva Path Ten Development Grounds” and they are: (41) Pramudita - joy ground; (42) Vimala - purity ground; (43) Prabhakari - enlightenment ground; (44) Arcismati - wisdom ground; (45) Sudurjaya - no difficulty ground; (46) Abhimukhi - open way ground; (47) Duramgama - proceeding afar ground; (48) Acala - unperturbed ground; (49) Sadhumati - discriminatory wisdom ground; (50) Dharma megha - dharma cloud ground (see Perfect Enlightenment and Wondrous Enlightenment).
chapterbody 50: A long sacred mantra taught by Shakyamuni Buddha found in The Great Compassion Dharani Sutra.
chapterbody 51: Buddha (Sanskrit, literally “Awakened One”)
chapterbody 52: Great Perfection (Dzogchen)
chapterbody 53: The term is typically used to refer to the historical Buddha, Shakyamuni Buddha. In Mahayana Buddhism, the term is not restricted to just Shakyamuni Buddha, but may refers to anyone who is enlightened.
chapterbody 54: Buddha-essence (or Buddha-nature)
chapterbody 55: The inherent nature of all sentient beings. All sentient beings have the potential to awaken their buddha-nature and become buddhas.
chapterbody 56: The supreme tantra of the Nyingma School. Cutting Through Resistance (khregs-chod) and All-Surpassing Wisdom (thodgal) are two of principal practices of Great Perfection. Adepts of Great Perfection have been known for demonstrating rainbow body accomplishment upon their passing, in which their flesh bodies gradually dissolves into five-colored rainbow light leaving hair and nails as the only physical remains.
chapterbody 57: Guanyin Bodhisattva
chapterbody 58: Chinese name for Avalokitesvara Bodhisattva - the Bodhisattva of mercy and compassion.
chapterbody 59: Guru
chapterbody 60: Two Sanskrit words - “gu” means darkness and “ru” means light. Therefore, a guru is one who can lead the student from darkness to light - from ignorance to wisdom. In Vajrayana Buddhism, the guru (teacher) is the first and foremost element of one’s level of accomplishment. This teacher gives the practitioner the lineage blessing of all past lineage gurus. The guru has also learned, practiced and attained accomplishments in his or her teachings. With the guidance of an authentic guru, one may more quickly reach enlightenment.
chapterbody 67: A wrathful form of Guru Rinpoche, Padmasambhava.
